sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit: The Forge Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member offboarding & win-back**: no documented process for exit interviews, pause reasons, re-engagement campaigns, or churn analysis by cohort
- **Complaint & incident handling**: no escalation path for client injuries, coach misconduct, facility damage, or billing disputes
- **Financial close & reporting**: no monthly reconciliation SOP, P&L review cycle, or cash-flow forecasting process
- **Pricing & package changes**: no SOP for annual price increases, promotional discounts, package launches, or grandfathering existing members
- **Coach performance review & development**: no formal quarterly/annual appraisal process, upskilling plan, or underperformance management
- **Crisis & emergency response**: no documented protocol for injuries mid-class, facility breakdown, breach of data, or staffing emergencies
- **Retention metrics tracking**: no SOP for cohort retention dashboards, LTV calculation, or churn prediction triggers
- **New program/class launch**: no go-live checklist for new offerings (bootcamp format, class time, new coach debut)

---

**Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding (22 SOPs)**: likely missing handoff from inquiry → payment setup → first class experience; check for CRM workflow, payment failure recovery, and new-joiner retention interventions (crucial in week 1–4)
- **Coach SOPs (17 SOPs)**: review whether they cover class cancellation protocols, substitute cover, member feedback loops, or behaviour standards (professionalism, boundaries, social media); likely no "coach gets sick" emergency cover plan
- **Marketing (10/12)**: Email Newsletter & Blog Post SOPs are acknowledged gaps; also check whether Client Referral Incentive, Seasonal Campaign, or Partner/Corporate Sales workflows exist
- **Facilities

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## REVENUE: URGENT — FILL THE EXPANDED SPACE

- **Studio expansion is underutilised.** You've gone from 85–108 active clients (Sept–June) and capacity should have doubled post-April relocation. June has 147 members but revenue is *down* 12.2% month-on-month (£20.2k → £17.7k) and profit collapsed 84% (£5.9k → £0.9k). AR/M (average revenue per member) has tanked to £120.48 — your lowest on record. **This is your single biggest problem.** You're not pricing the new capacity or upselling existing members.
- **Raise membership price immediately.** At £120/member/month average, you're underpricing. Comparable Belfast women's gyms charge £35–45/month. If your base tier is below £40, move to £42–45 and introduce a premium tier (personal training add-on, nutrition coaching, boutique class packs) targeting the 147 members. A £5 rise across the board = +£735/month; a 20% premium upsell to 30 members = +£1.8k/month.
- **Fix churn in May/June.** Attrition spiked to 5.74% in May (vs 0% in June, which is unrealistic — likely data lag). The relocation disrupted retention; you need a win-back campaign and a structured onboarding process for new members. Current trend is volatile; stabilise at 2% or lower.
- **Monetise the bigger studio.** Add revenue lines: class packages (10-class punch cards at 15% premium), personal training (even at £30/session, 3 sessions/month across 20 members = +£1.8k), merchandise, nutrition/supplement retail. Q2 (Apr–Jun) averaged £18.5k; you should be hitting £22–24k by month 4 of the new space.

---

## EXPENSES: NON-PEOPLE COSTS ARE OUT OF CONTROL

- **Non-people expenses are the crisis.** May: £9.2k (45% of revenue). June: £9.4k (53% of revenue). Compare to pre-relocation baseline (Sept–Nov: avg £4.6k or 33% of revenue). Even accounting for a bigger premises, June non-people costs are 2× what they should be. **Audit immediately:** utilities, rent, insurance, supplies, equipment maintenance. Are you being double-billed on rent during

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 56% of the total coaching volume—particularly 44 PTs and 20 body comp scans. Redistribute some 1:1 work (PTs and scans) to Grace and Eilis to bring JoJo down to ~100 sessions and protect her from burnout.

• **Grace is underutilised at 52 sessions.** She's only coaching Mondays but has capacity for more. Extend her to Wednesday or Thursday (keeping Mondays as her main day) to pick up additional Build: Lower and Elevate classes—these play to her strength and free up JoJo.

• **Eilis is under-used and under-stretched.** At 48 sessions, she has room to grow. Assign her more Elevate and Plate classes on Wed/Thu/Fri, plus increase her 1:1 PT and body comp scan load. This builds her confidence on steadier paces before any future Tuesday work.

• **Body comp scans are over-dependent on JoJo (20 of 40 total).** Train Eilis and Grace to share these 1:1s more evenly—aim for ~13–14 each. This is lower-skill, high-revenue work ideal for load-balancing.

• **Build: Upper and Circuits classes lack backup cover.** JoJo runs most of these. Get Grace or Eilis certified/confident on Circuits, and rotate Build: Upper so it's not a single-point-of-failure if JoJo is unwell.

• **Proposed shift:** Grace +2 classes/week (Wed/Thu), Eilis +3–4 1:1 slots/week + 1 extra group class. JoJo cuts ~25–30

class_stats.suggestion:
**Move underperforming classes to peak days.** Schedule Engine, Capacity, and Barbelle during Wednesday and Tuesday slots when overall gym traffic is highest. This gives these classes better visibility and captures members already in the gym.

**Consolidate or replace very low-attendance classes.** Barbelle, Strongevity, and Games Night are consistently drawing under 15 members. Consider merging these into hybrid sessions or replacing them with variations of your top performers (Build classes, Elevate).

**Add extra Build: Upper and Lower sessions on Wednesday/Tuesday.** These are your strongest classes with consistent demand. Adding a second slot on your busiest days will capture demand that might be going unmet due to capacity limits.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your coaches first — map what they ACTUALLY deliver**
Don't design on a blank page. Sit down with your top 2-3 coaches this week and have them walk through a real client journey: what happens on day one, week four, month three? Capture the exact services they're delivering now (small group sessions, form checks, nutrition advice, etc.). This prevents you building a menu that doesn't match reality.

**2. Price by anchoring to your best-paying clients, not costs**
Ask your current highest-retention members what they'd pay for different tiers. You'll discover members value *consistency of access* (unlimited vs. limited classes) and *personal attention* (group vs. semi-private coaching) way more than you think. Build 2-3 tiers around this, not around gym overhead.

**3. Create two separate visuals: one for coaches, one for members**
Coach version = operational checklist (what's included in each tier, what requires upsell, red flags for retention). Member version = benefits-focused one-pager with pricing, no jargon. Get these into Canva today — no fancy design needed, just clarity.

**4. Test pricing with your wait-list this week**
You likely have women interested but hesitant. Send them a quick poll: "Which option appeals most?" Show your 2-3 tier options with price ranges. Real data beats guessing — and you'll have early buyers ready to launch.

**5. Lock it in for 30 days, then iterate**
Once live, track which tier sells most and why. After month one, you'll know what to tweak. Don't over

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

• **Audit your current drop-off points first** — Use Google Analytics to identify exactly where visitors leave (homepage? pricing? class details?). Then pull your last 50 enquiry emails and map what questions they asked *before* converting. Build your page hierarchy around closing those specific gaps, not guessing.

• **Lead with the "women-only" positioning hard** — This is your differentiator. A visitor should understand in 6 seconds why this matters to her (safety, comfort, no performance anxiety, community). Make this the hero section. Test two versions: one emphasizing community, one emphasizing judgment-free training.

• **Create a tight FAQ section born from real objections** — Don't write generic FAQs. Contact your last 20 enquirers and ask "what almost stopped you joining?" (childcare? timing? intimidation? cost?). Build the FAQ around *actual* hesitations, not imaginary ones. Answer in plain language, not fitness jargon.

• **A/B test your CTA placement and messaging** — Run "Book a Free Taster Session" vs. "Start Your 2-Week Trial" vs. "Chat with a Coach (5 mins)." Track which gets clicked, which converts to actual visits. Change the winner every 4 weeks.

• **Simplify your offer stack immediately** — If you offer memberships, class passes, and PT packages, your page probably confuses people. Pick your #1 entry point (likely the taster session or starter membership) and make that the dominant path. Bury options 2 and 3 deeper.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Feedback System for The Forge — Execution Plan

• **Start with post-class check-ins, not forms.** Have instructors ask 2-3 verbal questions in the last 2 mins of class (e.g., "What would make this better?") — capture notes on a shared Google Sheet. Forms kill response rates; conversations get honest data while building rapport.

• **Make it incentive-driven from day one.** Members who submit feedback (verbal or written) get entered into a monthly prize draw (free month, guest pass, merchandise). This isn't bribery — it's how you get real participation instead of silence.

• **Assign ONE person ownership.** This isn't Grace's job. Delegate to your most engaged staff member to collate feedback weekly, flag patterns, and report back to you in a 10-min Friday call. Without clear ownership, it dies.

• **Close the loop publicly.** When you act on feedback (change class time, new equipment, etc.), announce it in your next newsletter: *"Based on member feedback, we're..."* People respond when they see their voice actually changes things.

• **Extract actionable insight ruthlessly.** You're not collecting opinions for a report — you need 3-5 concrete decisions per month. If feedback isn't pushing a specific decision, it's noise. Focus on retention risks and revenue opportunities only.

What's your biggest pain point right now — retention, class attendance, or churn reasons?

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Start with your highest-revenue/highest-churn service first**
Don't write all five in parallel—that's 6 months of procrastination. Pick whichever service generates the most revenue or loses the most members due to confusion. Write that one completely (client journey from signup to cancellation), then use it as your template/tone-setter for the others. This builds momentum and prevents inconsistent documentation.

**2. Interview 2-3 members in each category in the next 48 hours**
Don't write from your desk. Ask: "Walk me through your first week—what confused you? What did you wish someone had told you?" Their actual pain points become your SOP structure. This takes 1 hour of calls and saves you rewriting.

**3. Structure each SOP around the "failure moment" not the happy path**
Your Fuel Forward SOP shouldn't explain what it is—it should answer: "Member bought the wrong thing," "Member didn't see results by week 3," "Member didn't know how to redeem." Same for each service. This is what people actually need.

**4. Use a stupidly simple template: One-page per service**
Goal/What They Get → First 7 Days (Step-by-Step) → Common Problems + Fixes → When/How to Upgrade/Cancel. Video a 2-min walkthrough for each, post in member portal. Lengthy docs don't get read; short + visual do.

**5. Assign ownership immediately**
You can't write all five alone. Assign each SOP to the team member closest to that service (personal trainer owns PT SOP, etc.). Your

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar Execution for The Forge

**1. Use a free tool with zero learning curve**
Grab Buffer or Later's free tier — schedule directly to Instagram/Facebook without logging in manually each time. Grace batches content Sunday nights (90 mins max) for the week ahead. This removes the "what do I post today?" friction that kills consistency.

**2. Create a repeating 4-post template, not random content**
- **Monday:** Member transformation/progress (builds social proof, costs £0)
- **Wednesday:** Class highlight or instructor spotlight (5-min phone video, high engagement)
- **Friday:** Weekend motivation or class schedule reminder (drives bookings)
- **Sunday:** Community/behind-scenes or upcoming event teaser (keeps followers invested)

This removes decision-making. Grace isn't staring at a blank screen wondering what to post.

**3. Raid your existing assets first**
Pull photos/videos from class sessions, member testimonials, or staff training already happening. Don't create new content from scratch. One 30-second phone video of a packed Spin class = 2-3 repurposable posts with different captions.

**4. Set one owner rule: "Captions include a clear action"**
Not "Check out this class!" but "DM to book your free trial on Friday 6pm slot — 2 spots left." Every post should drive trial bookings, not just vanity metrics.

**5. Schedule a monthly refresh (30 mins)**
Last Friday of the month, Grace reviews what got engagement, tweaks next month's themes, and adds any new class launches or events. Prevents stale content without constant tinkering.

**Start this week

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Videography Session Scheduling — Execution Plan

• **Lock in the contract NOW** — Get Jordan on a formal retainer agreement specifying: 8-week cadence, 2-3 hour sessions, deliverables (how many cuts/edits), and turnaround time. This prevents "he's busy" excuses derailing your content pipeline. Cost it as a fixed line item so you stop treating it as optional.

• **Build your content calendar backwards** — Map what you're filming 2 weeks before each session (new class launch, member transformation story, equipment feature, seasonal campaign angle). Send Jordan the shot list 5 days prior. This eliminates vague "just capture something" sessions that waste his time and yours.

• **Schedule the NEXT session before he leaves** — Block the calendar immediately after each session wraps. Make it a habit: as Jordan's packing up, you're both confirming the next date. Gaps kill momentum; this ensures content flows steadily.

• **Assign ONE person on your team as the "content brief owner"** — They own coordinating with Jordan, gathering class schedules, flagging members who've agreed to appear, and managing the raw footage handoff. You delegate this or it collapses under your other priorities.

• **Plan for editing turnaround upfront** — Agree: does Jordan deliver rough cuts in 1 week? Edited/captioned in 2? Or are you editing in-house? Link this to your social posting calendar so content doesn't sit idle.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing 6-Week Trial Automations for The Forge

**The actual challenge here:** You likely have trial enquiries hitting you via multiple channels (Instagram DMs, website form, WhatsApp, email) with inconsistent follow-up timing and messaging. You're probably losing conversions to slow responses and one-off manual outreach.

**Concrete execution steps:**

• **Map your current leaks first (30 mins).** Track every trial enquiry from the past 2 weeks—what channel, how long until first reply, who followed up, what happened. You'll see where the breakdown is (my guess: initial response time and day-3 dropout).

• **Build a 3-email sequence in whatever CRM you use now** (Mailchimp, Acuity, even Gmail filters work). Email 1: Same-day confirmation + gym tour link/video + ask fitness goals. Email 2: Day 3 (if no reply) = social proof from current members. Email 3: Day 7 = limited-time conversion offer. Make them personal (mention their stated goals from the enquiry).

• **Automate the first touchpoint ruthlessly.** Set up automatic SMS or WhatsApp instant reply (via Twilio or similar) confirming you've got their enquiry + expected response time. This buys you credibility while you're busy.

• **Test one channel first.** Don't automate everything at once. Pick your biggest enquiry source (probably Instagram), get *that* system running flawlessly for 2 weeks, then layer in the others.

• **Track the ONE metric that matters:** % of trial enquiries who actually show up for their first session. If automations increase

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Role Checklists + KPIs — Execution Plan

**1. Start with outputs, not inputs**
Don't build checklists first. Ask JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah: "What does success look like for you weekly?" Capture their actual revenue/retention/operational impact. Then reverse-engineer what tasks drive those KPIs. This prevents busy-work checklists.

**2. Use a single shared spreadsheet (not documents)**
Create one live Google Sheet with tabs per person. Each tab: Task | Frequency | Owner | KPI it moves | Status. Non-negotiable: link every task to a measurable outcome (e.g., "Retention outreach" → "Member churn rate <3%"). Makes accountability visible and stops scope creep.

**3. Run a 30-min "role clarity sprint" this week**
Meet each person 1-on-1 for 20 mins. Ask: "What 5 things, if done weekly, would make the biggest dent in [their KPI]?" Write them down verbatim. Then validate together: "Is this realistic?" This avoids you guessing their capacity.

**4. Set KPI anchors to current performance + 10%**
If JoJo currently converts 20 leads/week, target 22. If Eilis retains 95%, target 96.5%. Realistic targets get done; moonshot ones get ignored. Review actual numbers each Monday (not aspirational ones).

**5. Lock it down Friday EOD next week**
Give all three the final version by Friday. They carry it on their phones. Review live in 15-min Monday standup. Done.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session — Execution Blueprint

• **Structure it as a 90-min working session, not a lecture.** 60 mins: you present SSO + 90-day plan with 2-3 specific wins you're targeting (e.g., "50 new members by March"). 30 mins: breakout by role (front desk, trainers, management) — each person writes down their 3 core metrics they own and one thing blocking them. Reconvene and call out each lane publicly so there's no ambiguity or overlap.

• **Make the SSO tangible with a visual one-pager** — literally one page with your Specific outcome (e.g., "£2k MRR growth"), the 3-4 quarterly initiatives driving it, and a simple visual linking each person's role to those initiatives. Hand it out. Make it reference-able, not a deck that gets forgotten.

• **Define "lanes" using outcomes, not job titles.** Don't say "Sarah runs marketing" — say "Sarah owns member acquisition: 12 new sign-ups/month." Don't say "Trainer team handles classes" — say "Trainers own retention: <5% monthly churn + class attendance 3x/week." People know what winning looks like immediately.

• **End with a single commitment round.** Popcorn-style: each person says aloud one thing they're committing to in the next 30 days tied to their lane. It's awkward, it works, and it kills the "I didn't know this mattered" excuse later.

• **Schedule a 20-min check-in for week 2.** Not to police them — to remove roadbl

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge (30-Day Sprint)

**1. Map actual time, not job titles (Day 1-2)**
Have each team member track their day in 30-min blocks for 3 days (spreadsheet, not memory). You'll spot the real bottlenecks—likely admin/scheduling eating up floor staff time, or one person owning all member retention calls. This beats guessing.

**2. Identify the "invisible load" (Day 3)**
Ask directly: "What takes your time that isn't in your job description?" You'll find unwritten expectations (someone always answering the phone, one person fixing tech issues). This stuff doesn't show up on paper but crushes people.

**3. Quick reassign, don't reorganize (Day 4-10)**
Move 2-3 specific tasks to underutilized people TODAY. Example: if your PT is doing member check-in calls, move that to your admin person. Don't wait for a "proper restructure"—test the shift immediately and adjust.

**4. Create a rotating "coverage role" for peak hours (Day 11-20)**
Overwhelmed at 6pm weekday rush? Rotate who "owns" that slot—one person leads floor, one handles admin/queries. Prevents the same person drowning while others coast.

**5. Weekly 10-min check-in every Friday (Week 2 onward)**
"Still overloaded?" Real answer = adjustment needed. Burnout from poor rebalance kills retention AND service quality. This is your metric to track.

**What's the biggest bottleneck right now—is it specific roles or time-of

# 10% Cost Cut for The Forge — Concrete Actions

**Audit your Big 3 first (they're 70%+ of costs)**
- Rent/lease: Call your landlord NOW with 6-month renewal data showing comparable gym rates in Belfast. Aim for 5% reduction by offering longer commitment or highlighting your occupancy rates. If they won't budge, seriously explore 1-2 alternative spaces—the threat alone moves landlords.
- Payroll: Map every staff hour against actual class attendance patterns. You likely have 1-2 shifts with 60%+ empty classes. Cut those hours, redistribute to peak times, hire one part-time instructor instead of maintaining slack capacity.
- Utilities: Get a commercial energy audit (often free from suppliers). Women's gyms run heavy on heating/hot water—negotiate a time-of-use tariff or switch suppliers. Real saving: 8-12%.

**Supplier squeeze (find 2-3% quick)**
- Contact your equipment maintenance, cleaning, and supplies vendors with a simple ask: "I'm getting 3 competitive quotes; beat their offer by 10% or I'm switching." Most will cut 5-8% to keep recurring revenue.
- Renegotiate your payment terms with suppliers—move from net-30 to net-60 to free up working capital (this isn't a cost cut but improves cash flow to absorb the 10%).

**Track it obsessively for 8 weeks**
- Build a simple spreadsheet with actual vs. budgeted costs by category, updated weekly. Grace, this keeps you accountable and shows staff you're serious—morale stays higher when people see purposeful cuts, not random

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs — The Forge

**Here's how Grace executes this TODAY:**

• **Pick 5 KPIs max, not 10.** For a gym: Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR), churn rate, cost per acquisition, average member lifetime value, and cash runway. Anything else dilutes focus. Track these in a single Google Sheet updated by the 3rd of each month.

• **Pull actuals at close of business on the last day of month.** Don't wait a week. Grace needs to see: how many cancellations happened, which payment method failed (revealing billing friction), what the member acquisition cost was THIS month vs. last. Speed = faster decisions.

• **Set one decision threshold per KPI.** Example: "If churn hits 8%, we pause paid ads and run a retention campaign." This stops her sitting on bad numbers. When MRR drops, she acts immediately on what's driving it (pricing? class quality? comms?).

• **Compare month-on-month AND vs. target.** A 5% revenue bump looks good until she sees she needed 12% to hit quarterly goals. Side-by-side columns show the gap clearly — this triggers faster pivots.

• **Block 30 mins mid-month for a "data check-in" with herself.** Not a formal review. She's looking early at trends (are new members stalling? Are renewals dropping?), so she can course-correct before month-end, not after.

What's the biggest KPI concern right now — revenue stalling or retention?

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution Plan

• **Get your raw numbers TODAY:** Pull last 12 months of membership revenue (by tier), class attendance, personal training income, and operating costs into a single spreadsheet. Don't wait for perfect data — use what you have. You need a baseline before you can forecast anything.

• **Build a simple 3-scenario model (not complex):** Create LOW, BASE, and HIGH case scenarios for next 12 months. Base case assumes current member retention + 5% new sign-ups monthly. LOW case drops to 2% growth. HIGH case assumes you hit a specific marketing initiative (e.g., corporate partnerships, referral program). This gives Grace decision-making clarity, not false precision.

• **Identify your ONE financial lever:** Is it membership churn rate, average class attendance per member, or personal training upsell? Calculate what happens if that metric improves 10%. This reveals where to actually spend energy vs. vanity metrics.

• **Set a monthly check-in rhythm (first Friday):** 15 minutes comparing actuals vs. forecast. When reality diverges, you'll spot cash flow problems or opportunities 60 days early instead of in crisis mode. Use a Google Sheet shared with your accountant.

• **Front-load the hard assumption:** What membership price and headcount do you need to hit break-even if costs stay flat? Lock this number down now — it's the one variable that drives everything else and stops you chasing shiny growth tactics that don't move the needle.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**Get the data structure right first**
- Pull last 3 months of revenue by service type (use your POS/booking system exports). You need: gross revenue per class type, PT sessions, and each add-on (smoothies, merchandise, challenges). Don't get bogged down in perfect categorization — "group classes," "1-on-1 PT," "retail/services" is enough to start.

**Calculate the real cost per service, ruthlessly**
- Class costs = studio rent allocation (total rent ÷ hours available) + instructor pay + music/tech. PT costs = instructor pay + space allocation only (no rent doubled). Add-ons = COGS + 15% handling overhead. The key: use *actual* instructor rates you're currently paying, not market rates. This tells you what's actually profitable *for you*.

- **Skip trying to allocate fixed costs perfectly** — you'll waste weeks debating methodology. Just isolate direct/variable costs per service. A PT session is more profitable when you see it costs £15-20 to deliver but brings in £40-60.

**Find your profit outliers quickly**
- Which service has the highest margin %? Which brings the most total profit volume? These aren't always the same (a low-margin class might still drive profit if you run 10/week). Identify the 1-2 services you should be doubling down on and the ones that are margin-killers.

**Act on one insight this week**
- Once you see the numbers, you'll likely spot something obvious (e.g., "PT is 60% margin but we're only doing 3 sessions/week" or

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations: Concrete Actions

**1. Pull your full automation map in 90 minutes**
Log into your CRM/email platform (Mailchimp, HubSpot, whatever you use). Export or screenshot every active automation—especially ones tied to enquiries, trial bookings, and member onboarding. Look for overlaps: two welcome sequences? Duplicate trial reminder emails? Kill the redundant ones immediately; members seeing double messages = unprofessional.

**2. Test the broken link in your funnel**
You likely have a leak between "enquiry submitted" → "trial booked" → "shows up for trial." Have a friend submit an enquiry form today. Track what happens: Does she get a confirmation email within 2 hours? Does a trial slot actually book in your system? Does she get a reminder 24h before? If any step fails or takes 6+ hours, that's lost conversions. Fix the slowest link first.

**3. Audit your trial-to-member conversion trigger**
Once someone *completes* a trial, what happens? They should get a "here's how to join" email within hours, not days. Check if that automation actually fires based on attendance data or if staff must manually trigger it. If manual, automate it NOW—Grace's time is too valuable.

**4. Identify one person accountable for this**
Assign a team member (or yourself this week) to own automation health monthly. Spend 30 mins the first Friday of each month checking for dead automations or stalled sequences. Without ownership, duplicates creep back in.

**Result:** A clean, fast enquiry→trial→member pipeline that doesn't rely on manual follow

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge: Concrete Execution

**Task 1: Membership Renewal Reminders**
- Use Zapier + ChatGPT to auto-generate personalized renewal emails (pulls member name, join date, class preferences from your system). Set it to trigger 30 days before expiry, segment by membership tier. Time saved: 2-3 hours/month. Start: Connect your membership software to Zapier today.

**Task 2: New Member Onboarding Sequences**
- Build a 7-email welcome series in Make.com that automatically sends based on signup date—class booking tips, facility tour video links, buddy assignments. No writing required if you record voice templates once. Cuts your manual follow-up by 80%. Time saved: 1.5 hours/week.

**Task 3: Class Cancellation + Reschedule Notifications**
- ChatGPT API + your booking system = instant SMS/email to affected members suggesting alternative classes. Removes the admin headache when instructors go sick. Time saved: 30 mins per incident (probably 2-3x/month).

**Quick start:** Pick **Task 1 first**—lowest friction, highest ROI. Spend 2 hours this week setting up Zapier integration and testing. You'll free up 8+ hours monthly immediately.

What's your current booking/membership software? That determines the specific connector.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution for The Forge Website

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY (free via Let's Encrypt)** — Your website needs HTTPS. If you're on WordPress, use a plugin like Wordfence that handles this automatically. This takes 30 mins max and is non-negotiable for member trust + Google rankings.

• **Audit what member data you're collecting right now** — Check your contact forms, payment pages, booking system. If you're storing card details directly (you shouldn't be), move to Stripe or Square immediately. Document what you touch so you know what to protect.

• **Install one solid security plugin and lock it down** — Wordfence or Sucuri for WordPress. Enable two-factor authentication for your admin login. Change default usernames to something random. This stops 95% of basic attacks without costing you.

• **Set up automated backups to cloud storage (not your server)** — Use UpdraftPlus or equivalent. Schedule daily backups. Test one restoration manually to confirm it actually works. If you get hit by ransomware, you recover in hours instead of losing everything.

• **Create a basic password policy for your team** — Everyone accessing the admin area uses unique, strong passwords stored in Bitwarden (free). Require password changes every 90 days. Document who has access and why.

**Real talk:** This isn't about being paranoid. Your members' payment info and personal data are your liability. Spending 4-5 hours now on this saves you thousands in breach costs or reputation damage later.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

• **Lead quality has collapsed.** You've gone from £6 to £20 cost-per-lead in two months while lead volume has dropped 70% (69 → 21). This isn't a spend problem; something in your creative, targeting, or offer is now attracting the wrong people—or repelling the right ones.

• **Close rate tells the real story.** April's 29% close rate (20/69) vs June's 48% (10/21) looks backwards. You're getting fewer leads but closing a higher *percentage*—which means the drop in CPA is masking a deeper problem: you've shifted from high-volume, qualified leads to a smaller, slightly warmer pool. You're optimising for the wrong metric.

• **Profit per lead is now £73 (£1538÷21), down 67% from April (£1533÷69 = £22/lead).** Even with better close rate, you're making far less profit because the baseline is so broken. The trial offer and funnel are working; the top-of-funnel is not.

---

**Actions**

1. **Pull creative and audience audit immediately.** What changed between May and June? Did you pause certain ad sets, refresh creative (and if so, what?), or shift targeting? April's performance suggests you had a winning combo—find what you turned off or changed and A/B test reverting it. If nothing changed deliberately, Meta's algorithm may have degraded your audience definition; rebuild Lookalike audiences from your April converters, not your full lead list.

2. **Test three new creative angles in parallel.** June's creative is likely fatigued or mis-targeted. Run: (a) social proof angle (member transformations, before/afters, testimonial video), (b) pain-point angle (time poverty, intimidation, isolation—not just "fitness"), (c) FOMO/urgency angle (limited trial slots, cohort-based). Keep budget small (£80–100/week per angle) and measure against your April benchmarks. If any hits sub-£12 CPA, scale it.

3. **Tighten targeting to April's sweet spot.** You likely over-broad
